one_line_takeaway: A POMDP-grounded framework (PSRL) that separates true-state safety from reward and delivers the first sound/complete-conditional algorithm (TASC) to certify RL safety under bounded adversarial observation perturbations, with a hybrid adversarial-training scheme that best balances certified safety, nominal reward, and interpretable state prediction.
key_finding: TASC certifies safety while exploring a tiny fraction of the search tree: below 5% of nodes in all cases and often under 1%, dropping to fewer than 0.0001 of all nodes for Tv=10 and fewer than 0.00001 for Tv=15; the M=500 node cap was rarely saturated and did not affect reported medians. On training, PSRL-HYBRID gives the best overall balance of high nominal reward AND high certified safety across all three scenarios, whereas plain PSRL (Vanilla) and AT yield essentially no meaningful safety certification (and AT often hurts nominal reward); RADIAL and PSRL-AT are inconsistent (best in some scenarios, poor in others). For state-prediction MSE under an eps=1/255 perturbation (bounds via beta-CROWN), PSRL-AT and PSRL-HYBRID beat the alternatives, with PSRL-AT notably the best, giving the PSRL methods an interpretability advantage.
core_contribution: The paper reframes adversarial robustness of reinforcement learning around SAFETY (never visiting unsafe true states) rather than cumulative reward. It makes three own contributions: (1) PSRL (Partially-Supervised Reinforcement Learning), a compositional framework that models the environment as a deterministic POMDP whose true low-dimensional interpretable state s is observable at TRAINING time but not at decision time; a policy is built as pi(o)=pi_s(g(o)), composing a supervised perception module g (image -> vehicle positions, l2 regression loss) with a Q-learning semantic policy pi_s over predicted state (DDQN). (2) TASC (Tree-based Adversarial Safety Certification), described by the authors as the first algorithm for certifying safety of RL policies under adversarial input perturbations; it computes a provable lower bound eps-bar on the largest perturbation budget under which no unsafe state can be reached over a finite verification horizon, by expanding a sparse action tree and using per-observation certified action sets. (3) Two new PSRL-aware adversarial training methods, PSRL-AT and PSRL-HYBRID, that exploit the compositional architecture, plus formal soundness/completeness results (Proposition 2: if the per-observation action-set certifier CERT is sound then TASC is sound; if CERT is also complete, TASC is gamma-complete with gamma = max gap in the eps schedule).
threat_model: A test-time evasion adversary perturbs the agent's high-dimensional observations. At each timestep the true observation o=f(s) is replaced by o'=o+delta with the perturbation bounded in an lp eps-ball (||delta_t||_p <= eps, p in {2, infinity}). The adversary controls a sequence of perturbations Delta over the finite POMDP horizon and succeeds if it drives the policy into an unsafe true state (U intersect S_T(pi,Delta) != empty), e.g. a vehicle collision or coming within 1 unit of another vehicle. Safety is defined over the TRUE low-dimensional state, not the perturbable observation; the adversary cannot alter true dynamics, only perceptual inputs. The paper's stance is that adversarial encounters are rare, so the objective is to certify/maximize the safety budget rather than worst-case reward.
limitations: This arXiv version is a short/extended-abstract (10 pages) that defers the full TASC pseudocode, l2 results, and detailed quantitative tables to an external full version, so Figure 1 reports results only as un-annotated bar charts without exact per-scenario numbers. The approach rests on strong assumptions: the true state must be observable at training time and the transition tau(s,a) simulable at certification time; safety is checked against ground-truth state, so certification quality is coupled to perception accuracy. Experiments are confined to one simulator (highway-env) with a small predicted state (two nearest vehicles) and small budgets ({1,2,3}/255); no real sensor data. Certification soundness/completeness inherit from the underlying CERT primitive (alpha,beta-CROWN / median smoothing), and gamma-completeness is only up to the discretization gap of the eps schedule. Policies condition only on the latest observation, not full history.

quantitative_results: TASC explores below 5% of tree nodes in all cases, and under 1% in many cases
For Tv=10, TASC explores fewer than 0.0001 fraction of all nodes; for Tv=15, fewer than 0.00001 fraction
Verification horizons Tv in {5, 10, 15}; node exploration cap M=500 (rarely saturated)
DDQN Q-network training: 2000 episodes (Highway), 3000 episodes (Twoway), 50,000 steps (Exit)
Adversarial training: 40,000 steps with eps ramped linearly over first 25,000 steps to targets {1,2,3}/255
State-prediction MSE bounds computed under eps=1/255 via beta-CROWN (avg err, avg err ub, avg err lb)
Reward shaping: +0.1 rightmost lane, +0.5 high speed (20-30), -1 unsafe/collision, +1 successful exit; unsafe = collision or within 1 unit of another vehicle
baselines_compared: AT (standard PGD-based adversarial training on actor Q)
RADIAL (Oikarinen et al. 2021, IBP-based, adapted by backpropagating through composite pi(o)=pi_s(g(o)))
